user_prompt: so `void loopTask(void *pvParameters)`, under some configurations, will never ever call taskYIELD?
agent_reply: Yes, in this `main.cpp`, `loopTask()` itself never directly calls `taskYIELD()`.
- **UNICORE build:** it may call `vTaskDelay(5)` every ~2 seconds via `yieldIfNecessary()`.
- **Dual-core build:** that block is compiled out, so `loopTask()` has no built-in yield/delay at all.

But that does **not** mean the task can never be preempted. FreeRTOS can still preempt it on tick interrupts if `CONFIG_FREERTOS_HZ` / preemption are enabled, and calls inside your `loop()` may block/yield/delay. It just means Arduino’s wrapper loop does not voluntarily yield for you on dual-core ESP32.
So if your `loop()` is a tight CPU-bound loop on dual core, it can keep its core busy indefinitely unless your code calls `delay()`, `vTaskDelay()`, `taskYIELD()`, blocks on I/O/queues, etc.

user_prompt: Add a new view to `lib\FlyGuiViews` called `SplashView`

agent_reply: It uses new `FLYGUI_VIEW_SPLASH = 0`, streams `/splash.png` from LittleFS to the full `320x240` display, waits `800ms`, then hands off to `FLYGUI_VIEW_MAIN`. If the image or filesystem load fails, it clears to black before handing off.

user_prompt: src/main.cpp:13:24: warning: ignoring attribute 'section (".rtc.data.2")' because it conflicts with previous 'section (".rtc.data.0")' [-Wattributes]
src/main.cpp:14:24: warning: ignoring attribute 'section (".rtc.data.3")' because it conflicts with previous 'section (".rtc.data.1")' [-Wattributes]
can you explain what the warnings mean? I was told that RTC_DATA_ATTR makes it store it in a slow persistent RAM region that is kept between resets if power is not lost
agent_reply: Yes, your understanding of `RTC_DATA_ATTR` is basically right: on ESP32 it places the variable into RTC memory, which can survive many reset types as long as power is not removed.
The warning is about applying `RTC_DATA_ATTR` in two places:

`RTC_DATA_ATTR` expands to a GCC section attribute, something like “put this symbol in `.rtc.data...`”. GCC is seeing one section attribute on the `extern` declaration and then another section attribute on the actual definition. Because ESP’s macro expansion gives them distinct internal section names, GCC says: “these declarations disagree, so I’m ignoring one of the attributes.”

The important practical bit: the variables are still likely ending up in RTC memory because the actual definitions in `main.cpp` have `RTC_DATA_ATTR`. But the header declarations should not carry that attribute. The clean pattern is:
So the warning does not mean RTC RAM does not work. It means the attribute was redundantly attached to both the declaration and definition, and GCC is complaining about the mismatch.

user_prompt: does M5 stuff need some sort of poll? Remember when we had problems using the Vbus signal for charging? does that call only read from a cache and need a poll to update?
agent_reply: Yes: M5 as a whole needs polling via `M5.update()`, especially for buttons/touch. In this project that already happens inside `FlyGui::poll()`, and `main.cpp` calls `gui->poll()` in the main loop.

For the charging/VBUS question specifically: `M5.Power.isCharging()` does **not** look like a cached value that only updates after `M5.update()`. In M5Unified it routes into the PMIC-specific implementation, like AXP192/AXP2101/IP5306, and reads the power chip state directly.
The cache to watch out for is **our** `BattTracker`: it reads M5 power state in `BattTracker::poll()`, but I don’t see `BattTracker::poll()` being called anywhere right now. So:
- `BattTracker::isCharging()` / `BattTracker::status()` may be stale after init unless `BattTracker::poll()` runs.
- The current FlyGui top bar bypasses `BattTracker` and calls `M5.Power.getBatteryLevel()` plus `M5.Power.isCharging()` directly.

So if the charging icon is wrong, I would not blame missing `M5.update()` first. More likely suspects are `isCharging()` vs “USB/VBUS present” semantics, top-bar redraw throttling/state caching, or some code path using stale `BattTracker` values.

user_prompt: I tried to pair, I confirmed it on my phone, then I saw the device rebooted and `rst:0x10 (RTCWDT_RTC_RESET),boot:0x17 (SPI_FAST_FLASH_BOOT)` on my terminal
agent_reply: That reboot makes sense now. The likely culprit was our pairing-complete path doing too much inside `ESP_BT_GAP_AUTH_CMPL_EVT`.

Specifically, the GAP callback was calling `g_host_list.insert()`, and that calls `BtManager::isBonded()`, which queries the GAP bonded-device list. That is re-entering Bluetooth/GAP from inside a Bluetooth/GAP callback, right at authentication completion. Very plausible RTC watchdog stall.
- GAP auth callback now only copies the paired device data and sets a pending flag.
- `BtManager::poll()` handles the Bluetooth-side post-pair work later: insert into BtManager’s internal list, fire paired callback, close pairing window, return state to idle.
- `main.cpp` now registers a paired callback and defers app-side work to the main loop: insert into the JSON-backed `bt_host_list`, save `bluetooth.json`, and return from `ConnWaitingView`.
This should stop the watchdog reset and also make a successful pair persist into the UI’s host list.

user_prompt: the problem with waiting for audio is that what if we want to start talking first?
should I just do a test to see what the actual event sequence are? add more verbose logging to help determine this
agent_reply: Added event-sequence logging without changing the recording trigger behavior.
- `queueing Bluetooth recording start from state: X`
- First few and then 1/sec `hfp incoming audio callback`
- First few and then 1/sec `hfp outgoing audio callback`
This should tell us whether “talk first” is viable by watching for outgoing mic callbacks before any incoming speaker audio.

For the test, the most interesting sequence is whether you get `AudioAvailable`, then `hfp outgoing audio callback` even if nobody on the phone side is talking. If yes, starting recording at `AudioAvailable` is enough.

user_prompt: I am asking for a big change to both `WifiManager` and `BtHostList`
Previously, the WiFi credentials lived in a JSON file on the microSD card. This is of course vulnerable.
I want all configurations to live inside NVS when the build has security enabled
I have started to sprinkle the flag "BUILD_WITH_SECURITY" in a lot of places.

So instead of a linked list that is loaded from JSON, we will use structures with a fixed number of entries. The strings are fixed length instead of allocated on demand. 8 entries for everything should be more than enough.
* make sure the string lengths make sense for the data it needs to hold
* add constants for the string lengths so the code doesn't have hard coded numbers
* implement the loading and saving of the structures through NVS
* make everything is still compatible with current code

The bluetooth part of this has some added features, like a user customized name vs reported name storage, and a last used time. For last used time, only if the storage is full and pairing happens, the oldest unbonded entry is overwritten. If all entries are bonded, then the oldest bonded entry is overwritten.

there shall be two implementations of `loadFromMicroSd`, with `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY`, it loads the JSON but it loads it into NVS if existing, if not existing, no error is shown. Without `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY`, it loads as it does now.
agent_reply: Implemented the secure NVS-backed paths for both managers.
- [WifiManager.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.h:7) now defines fixed string-length constants and 8-slot secure storage for stations, APs, and cloud endpoints.

- [WifiManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp:765) now loads NVS first under `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY`, optionally imports `/wifi.json` into NVS if present, and treats missing JSON as non-fatal.

- [BtHostList.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.h:8) now defines fixed Bluetooth host storage, including display name, custom name, reported name, bond state, last-used time, and 8 slots.
- [BtHostList.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.cpp:444) now imports `/bluetooth.json` into NVS under `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY`, with missing JSON non-fatal.

- [BtHostList.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.cpp:797) now supports secure fixed-slot insert/remove. If full, pairing overwrites oldest unbonded first, otherwise oldest bonded.
- Kept compatibility fields like `ssid`, `password`, `name`, and `next_node` so current UI/test/main code still works.
- Normal `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest` compiles touched C++ and still fails only at the existing IRAM overflow: `.iram0.text` overflowed by `2052 bytes`.

- Direct secure-branch compile with `-DBUILD_WITH_SECURITY` passed for `WifiManager.cpp`, `BtHostList.cpp`, `main.cpp`, `CloudUpload.cpp`, `ScrollView.cpp`, `BluetoothManager.cpp`, and `test_bootreadfiles.cpp`.

user_prompt: I want to run `void test_sdcard()` to test performance of the recorder with encryption enabled
make sure everything is wired so that it works with encryption actually built and active
agent_reply: Wired `test_sdcard()` so it should actually test encrypted recorder performance now.
- `setup()` now calls `all_init()` before bring-up tests, so NVS/M5/gui basics exist before `Aegis` and SD setup.
- installs a deterministic test master key only if no key already exists,
- forces `AudioFileRecorder::setPurePcmMode(false)` so it does not bypass encryption.
- `AudioFileRecorder` now includes `Aegis` by relative path so PlatformIO library discovery works.
- `BtHostList.h` and `WifiManager.h` now include `conf.h`, so their security-mode structs match their `.cpp` files.
Build passed. I restored the generated `src/version.c` bump afterward.
